coredata.dc:title: CDs/Fe2O3 composite catalyst and its effect on the thermal decomposition and ignition combustion of TKX-50
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhen
Zhao, Fengqi
Zhang, Ming
Jiang, Yifan
Qin, Zhao
Qu, Wengang
Chen, Chao
Zhang, Jiankan
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (CDs) were obtained by oxidation etching of coal pitch powder with peroxic acid. The CDs/Fe2O3 composites were in-situ prepared by hydrothermal method. The composition, morphology and structure of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 were analyzed by TEM/HRTEM, XRD, EDS and XPS. The effects of CDs, Fe2O3 and CDs/Fe2O3 on the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 were studied by DSC-MS-FTIR and in-situ-solid FTIR. The three materials decreased the peak temperature of TKX-50 by 12.1, 33.9 and 39.0℃ (under the warming rate of 10℃·min−1). The CDs/Fe2O3 showed best catalytic performance. The carboxyl group contained on the surface of CDs adsorbed NH3, inhibiting the formation of by-decomposition products ABTOX. Besides assimilating NH3 and restraining the agglomeration of Fe2O3, the CDs/Fe2O3 provided more active sites for decomposition which reduce the decomposition and activation energy of TKX-50 and promoted the reaction. Furthermore, CDs/Fe2O3 reduced the ignition delay time of TKX-50 and significantly increased the flame propagation speed and flame area. Therefore, CDs and its compound CDs/Fe2O3 have excellent application prospects in solid propellant combustion catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CDs/Fe2O3 composite
TKX-50
Thermal decomposition
Combustion catalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology, dimension, constitution and construction of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3
Analysis of the catalytic performance of TKX-50

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
National Major Science and Technology Projects of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs; (c) size distribution curves of CDs; (d-f) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs/Fe2O3.
XRD results of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3.
EDS mapping images of CDs/Fe2O3 samples.
XPS analysis of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3.
DSC curves of TKX-50, TKX-50 + CDs,TKX-50+Fe2O3, and TKX-50+CDs/Fe2O3.
MS spectrograms of TKX-50 before and after mixed with CDs, Fe2O3, and CDs/Fe2O3: NH3(m/z = 17), H2O(m/z = 18), HCN(m/z = 27), NH2(m/z = 16), NO(m/z = 30), CO2/N2O(m/z = 44), and N2/CO(m/z = 28).
Comparison of molar percentage of TKX-50 thermal decomposition gaseous products before and after catalyst addition.
FTIR spectrums of TKX-50, TKX-50+CDs, TKX-50+Fe2O3, TKX-50+CDs/ Fe2O3.
(a) Schematic diagram of the laser ignition device, (b) ignition delay time of the sample at different laser power densities.
Image of flame propagation during combustion of different samples at a power density of 190 W·cm−2.
Schematic diagram of thermal decomposition of TKX-50 and TKX-50/catalyst.
In-situ solid FTIR spectrums of TKX-50, TKX-50+CDs, TKX-50+Fe2O3, TKX-50+CDs/Fe2O3.
Non-isothermal reaction kinetic parameters of pure TKX-50 and TKX-50 mixed with CDs, Fe2O3, CDs/Fe2O3.

head.ce:title: CDs/Fe2O3 composite catalyst and its effect on the thermal decomposition and ignition combustion of TKX-50

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion Laboratory, Xi'an Modern Chemistry Research Institute, 168 Zhangba Road, Xi'an 710065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion Laboratory, Xi'an Modern Chemistry Research Institute, 168 Zhangba Road, Xi'an 710065, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon dots (CDs) were obtained by oxidation etching of coal pitch powder with peroxic acid. The CDs/Fe2O3 composites were in-situ prepared by hydrothermal method. The composition, morphology and structure of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 were analyzed by TEM/HRTEM, XRD, EDS and XPS. The effects of CDs, Fe2O3 and CDs/Fe2O3 on the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 were studied by DSC-MS-FTIR and in-situ-solid FTIR. The three materials decreased the peak temperature of TKX-50 by 12.1, 33.9 and 39.0℃ (under the warming rate of 10℃·min−1). The CDs/Fe2O3 showed best catalytic performance. The carboxyl group contained on the surface of CDs adsorbed NH3, inhibiting the formation of by-decomposition products ABTOX. Besides assimilating NH3 and restraining the agglomeration of Fe2O3, the CDs/Fe2O3 provided more active sites for decomposition which reduce the decomposition and activation energy of TKX-50 and promoted the reaction. Furthermore, CDs/Fe2O3 reduced the ignition delay time of TKX-50 and significantly increased the flame propagation speed and flame area. Therefore, CDs and its compound CDs/Fe2O3 have excellent application prospects in solid propellant combustion catalysts.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid propellant is the power source of aerospace and weapon equipment, and its performance directly affects the operational efficiency and survivability of military activities [1,2]. The adjustment of propellant performance with combustion catalyst is the key of propulsion technology. Combustion catalyst is the core component of solid propellant, which can effectively improve the burning rate and energy release efficiency. With the boost of national defense and engineering applications, it is particularly necessary to develop new high-efficiency combustion catalysts to further improve the combustion performance of propellants [3,4].

The 1,1′-dihydroxy-5,5′-ditetrazolium dihydroxy-ammonium salt (TKX-50) is a energetic material with excellent comprehensive properties, which has both high energy density and higher stability [5,6]. The polynitrogen heterocyclic structure of TKX-50 consists of a high potential energy. At the same time, compared with traditional energetic materials, TKX-50 gets rid of the restriction of nitro, and its sensitivity to thermal and mechanical is effectively reduced compared with cyclotetramethylene tetramine (HMX) and hexanitro hexaazaisowurtzitane (CL-20) [7,8]. In addition, TKX-50 contains halogen-free and produces less smoke during combustion, which has a good application prospect. TKX-50 is an important energetic component of future insensitive propellants, and its stable characteristics are expected to be well applied in new high energy insensitive propellants such as hydroxyl terminated polybutadiene (HTPE) propellants [9,10].

With the continuous development of national defense and the urgent need of engineering application, it is particularly important to improve the combustion performance of solid propellant. In order to boost the thermal decomposition efficiency of energetic materials, reduce the activation energy of thermal decomposition reaction, and further enhance the catalytic performance, it is necessary to develop new high-efficiency catalysts. At present, in the catalytic researches of TKX-50, nano-metal oxides have shown advantageous application prospect [11,12]. Due to the increase of the specific surface area and contact surface, the agglomeration problem inevitably exists. Therefore, using the high specific surface area of carbon materials to effectively compound metal oxides has become a new research hotspot [13–16]. At present, the dispersion has been improved to a certain extent by in-situ growth of supported metal and oxide particles on carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene oxide (GO) [17–19].

Carbon dots (CDs) are the new zero-dimensional nanometer material after CNTs and GO. The size of CDs is usually 2∼10 nm [20,21]. Compared with other carbon nanomaterials, CDs can be obtained in a wider range of ways (such as chemical oxidation, hydrothermal, pyrolysis, microwave assisted methods and so on). In addition, the studies have shown that CDs have better stability and dispersion, and the modification of surface groups is easier to achieve [22–24]. The unique electronic structure and energy level of CDs are expected to achieve excellent catalytic performance. At present, CDs have played an important role in the field of photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [25–27]. The introduction of CDs provides a new research direction for the synthesis of high efficiency solid propellant catalysts. The preparation method and functional modification of CDs can modify the surface group and structure, increase the active site and achieve targeted catalytic effect. By introducing CDs into the field of solid propellant combustion catalysts, the combination of CDs with metals and their oxides is expected to enable efficient catalysis in the field of solid propellants.

According to the research status of solid propellant combustion catalysts, CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 composites were prepared to meet the catalytic requirements of a new type of sensitive energetic material TKX-50. The morphology, size and composition of the composites were studied by TEM, HRTEM, XRD, EDS and XPS. The effects of CDs and its composites on the thermal decomposition products of TKX-50 were analyzed by DSC, MS-DSC and FTIR-DSC. According to the thermal decomposition temperature at different heating rates (5, 10, 15, 20℃·min−1), the kinetic parameters of thermal decomposition of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 before and after addition were calculated by Kissinger method and Owaza method. Finally, the effects of the catalyst on the ignition delay and flame propagation of TKX-50 were further analyzed. Based on the above experimental results, the possible mechanism of catalyst action on TKX-50 was discussed, and the application of CDs in solid propellant combustion catalysts was expanded.

All chemical reagents were analytical grade: coal pitch powder, ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), 30 % hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), formic acid, N-N'-dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethanol, which were all purchased from Aladdin Inc. (EA). TKX-50 was from Xi'an Modern Chemistry Research Institute.

The synthesis of CDs was mainly obtained by oxidation etching of coal pitch powder. At room temperature, formic acid was mixed with H2O2 in a certain proportion to obtain peroxic acid, then coal pitch powder was added to produce smaller crystalline carbon after oxidation etching, and CDs powder was obtained through purification [28].

In a typical experiment, 125 ml 98 % formic acid and 30 ml deionized water were added successively to each gram of coal pitch powder, and then 15 ml 30 % H2O2 was slowly added, and stirred at room temperature for 24 h to obtain a brown-black suspension. The unreacted impurities in the suspension were separated by extraction and filtration, and the brown carbon dots formic acid solution was obtained. The excess formic acid in the solution was removed by rotary evaporation and washed several times with deionized water until neutral. Then in a drying oven at 70 °C, the black powder was obtained as the CDs (Fig. 1 ).
CDs/Fe2O3 was prepared by in-situ growth of CDs and iron salts by hydrothermal reaction under high temperature and pressure.

A mixture of 150 ml DMF and water was added into 400 mg CDs and 3 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, where the volume mixing ratio of DMF and water was 3:1. The brown and black solution was obtained by mixing thoroughly. The solution was transferred to the inner lining of 200 ml high-pressure reactor, heated to 180℃ at the heating rate of 2.5℃·min−1, kept at constant temperature for 12 h, and cooled to room temperature after the reaction. The resulting brown mixture was centrifuged at a centrifugation rate of 6000 rpm to collect solid precipitates. The precipitates were repeatedly washed with ethanol and deionized water to make the clear liquid colorless. Last, the black solid was dried at 70℃ for 12 h (Fig. 2).

The surface morphology and size of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 were characterized by transmission electron microscopy and High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM/HRTEM QuanTA600), and the particle size distribution of CDs was statistically analyzed. The structure and composition of CDs, CDs/Fe2O3 were characterized by X-ray Diffraction (XRD). The valence electrons and compositions of the elements were analyzed by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS). The structure of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 was further characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR).

Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) was used to study the catalytic effects of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 on the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 (the catalyst content was 1:10 of TKX-50), and the DSC curves of each sample were studied at four different heating rates on 5, 10, 15, 20℃·min−1 (N2 flow rate was 25 ml·min−1, the sample mass was 0.5 ± 0.2 mg). In addition, at a heating rate of 10℃·min−1, the effects of different catalysts on the pyrolysis products of TKX-50 were compared by Differential Scanning Calorimetry-Mass Spectrometry-Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (DSC-MS-FTIR). The Kissinger and Ozawa methods were used to study the pyrolysis kinetics of TKX-50 before and after adding catalyst (equation (Eqs. (1) and (2)).
(1)

T m 2
)
= l n

E a

(2)

)

0.4567

= C

E a
is activation energy,
β m
is heating rate, R is universal gas constant, A is preexponential factor, C is constant and
T m
is peak temperature.

Under the radiation wavelength of 10.6 μm and the maximum power density of 500 W·cm−2, the ignition delay time and flame propagation speed before and after the addition of catalyst was further studied by using CO2 continuous laser (SLC 110) and high-speed camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, dimension, constitution and construction of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and HRTEM were used to analyze the morphological characteristics of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 nanomaterials (Fig. 3 ). The results of Fig. 3a and 3b showed that the particle size of CDs powder obtained by oxidation etching coal pitch powder ranged from 1 to 5 nm, with an average particle size of 3 ± 0.5 nm. The HRTEM result showed that CDs has a good crystalline property, and its lattice spacing was 0.238 nm, corresponding to the interplanar lattice spacing of the (111) crystal faces in the two-dimensional graphite carbon plane [28]. The HRTEM results of the CDs/Fe2O3 showed that the composite had two lattice fringes with different orientations, where 0.241 nm corresponded to the CDs and 0.362 nm corresponded to the (012) crystal face of iron oxide (PDF#01–1053). This meant that CDs and Fe2O3 successfully compounded together.

The powder diffraction results of CDs and CDs/Fe2O3 were shown in Fig. 4 . The results of XRD showed that CDs had a wide peak between 20° and 30°, corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphitic carbon [29]. In addition, there were 11 obvious diffraction peaks at 28.1°, 38.8°, 41.8°, 48.0°, 58.2°, 63.9°, 68.0°, 73.8°, 76.2°, 83.0° and 86.1° in the diffraction pattern of CDs/Fe2O3 composites. These respectively corresponded to the (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (122), (214), (300), (208) and (1010) crystal planes of Fe2O3 (JCPDS NO. 01–1053) with unit cell parameters of a = 5.028 Å, b = 5.028 Å and c = 13.73 Å. This showed that in the composite material obtained by hydrothermal method, Fe was mainly combined with CDs in the form of Fe2O3.

In order to further determine the combination of CDs and Fe2O3 to form a composite material, EDS tests were performed on the composite catalyst and the elemental composition of CDs/Fe2O3 was analyzed (Fig. 5 ). The results showed that the composite was irregularly near-spherical, and Fe, C and O elements were uniformly distributed, which indicated that CDs and Fe2O3 formed an overall structure with a size of 250∼300 nm. The uniform distribution of C element in the composite signified the effective binding of CDs and Fe2O3, which further explained the formation of CDs/Fe2O3.

The valence state of Fe in the composite was further verified by XPS (Fig. 6 ). In the CDs high-resolution XPS C 1 s spectrogram, there were three kinds of carbon bonding structures, which were located at 284.4 eV (CC/C = C), 284.8 eV (CO), 287.6 eV (O = COH). This showed that the surface of the CDs contained carboxyl groups. The XPS of the composite material showed C, O and Fe signals. There were three absorption peaks in the C1s spectrum: CC (284.6 eV), CO (285.8 eV), and O = COH (288.0 eV). This indicated that the hydroxyl group on the surface of CDs was retained after forming the composite material with Fe2O3. In the high-resolution XPS of Fe, there were three strong peaks at 710.4, 716.5 and 724.0 eV, corresponding to 2p3/2, satellite peak and 2p1/2 of Fe3+ respectively [30–33]. This signified that no REDOX reaction occurred during the preparation of CDs/Fe2O3, and the iron element was always present in the form of Fe3+. The binding energy analysis of XPS spectra confirmed the presence of Fe3+, which further indicated that the prepared composite was CDs/Fe2O3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the catalytic performance of TKX-50

ce:section.ce:para: It could be clearly seen from the Fig. 7 and Table 1 that the TLDP of the four samples showed a trend of gradual augment with the increase of the heating rate at different heating rates. The addition of CDs made the low temperature decomposition peak of TKX-50 shift to the left, while the high temperature decomposition peak shifted to the right. And the dominant position of low temperature decomposition did not change. At the heating rate of 10℃·min−1, the addition of CDs reduced the TLDP of TKX-50 from 244.7℃ to 232.6℃. When Fe2O3 and CDs/Fe2O3 were added, the low temperature decomposition peak of TKX-50 moved further to the left. Meanwhile, the high temperature decomposition peak of TKX-50 moved to the low temperature decomposition peak obviously and tended to coincide with the low temperature decomposition peak. Among them, CDs decreased the TLDP of TKX-50 to 232.6℃, Fe2O3 decreased the TLDP of TKX-50 to 210.8℃ and CDs/Fe2O3 further reduced the TLDP to 205.7℃. This showed that Fe2O3 had a very obvious catalytic effect on TKX-50, which could diminish the TLDP of TKX-50 by about 33.9℃. In addition, when Fe2O3 and CDs form a complex, TLDP was further lowered (about 38.9℃), which was more advantageous than the sole catalysis of CDs and Fe2O3. With the addition of catalyst, the TLDP order of different samples was TKX-50+CDs/Fe2O3 < TKX-50+Fe2O3 < TKX-50+CDs < TKX-50. In addition, with the addition of the catalyst, the decomposition heat of TKX-50 also changed. In the first stage decomposition process of pure TKX-50, the apparent decomposition heat was 1357.1 J·g −1. When CDs was added, the apparent heat of decomposition decreased slightly. When Fe2O3 and CDs/Fe2O3 were added, the apparent heat of decomposition climbed to 1412.9 and 1551.1 J·g −1, respectively. It showed that CDs/Fe2O3 can significantly increase the reaction heat of TKX-50, thus providing more energy and showing better performance.

According to the TLDP under the four heating rates, the Kissinger method and Ozawa method were used to calculate the thermal decomposition kinetic parameters of the non-isothermal decomposition reactions of the four samples (Table 1). The effect of the addition of catalyst on the apparent activation energy of TKX-50 at low temperature decomposition stage was further investigated. It could be seen from the Table 1 that the addition of CDs increased the decomposition and activation energy of TKX-50, while the addition of Fe2O3 effectively decreased the decomposition and activation energy of TKX-50. The addition of CDs/Fe2O3 further reduced the decomposition and activation energy of TKX-50 to 150.6 kJ·mol−1 (decreased by 40.3 kJ·mol−1). This meant that the addition of composite materials diminished the low temperature decomposition energy barrier of TKX-50, thus promoting the decomposition of TKX-50 at lower temperatures, which showed the good catalytic performance. After binding with CDs, CDs/Fe2O3 showed a better catalytic effect, which might be due to the fact that the dispersion of Fe2O3 was improved by the composite material, and the presence of interfacial energy and more active sites in different phases were conducive to further reaction. Moreover, the surface of CDs contained organic groups that could be used to adsorb small molecules formed by TKX-50 decomposition (such as NH3, N2, NO, N2O, HCN and so on), which promoted the interaction [29].

DSC-FTIR experiments further showed that the addition of catalyst to TKX-50 did not change the type of thermal decomposition products, but the intensity of infrared signal corresponding to different temperatures did change (Fig. 10 ). The four samples showed obvious infrared signals at 243℃ (TKX-50), 235℃ (TKX-50+CDs), 211℃ (TKX-50+Fe2O3) and 206℃ (TKX-50+CDs/Fe2O3), which were basically consistent with the DSC results. In the corresponding decomposition temperature range, the infrared signal of CO2 appeared at 2245 cm−1 and 668 cm−1, and the signal of N2O appeared at 1260 cm−1 and 1320 cm−1. In addition, 1558 cm−1, 3660 cm−1, 3760 cm-1 were the signals of H2O, the 2230 cm−1 and 717 cm−1 were HCN infrared signals. In addition, the signals of NO (2200 cm−1), NH2 (3463 cm−1) and tetrazonium ring (970 cm−1,1180 cm−1) were also corresponding [34,37]. Therefore, in the corresponding decomposition temperature interval, the decomposition product type did not change, but the strength was different, which further verified the DSC-MS results.

The study of ignition performance and flame structure of catalyst/energetic material is also an important index to evaluate catalytic performance. The study of ignition combustion characteristics of materials is of great scientific significance and practical application value for revealing combustion reaction mechanism and improving ignition performance [38]. The ignition is the beginning of the burning process, which is accompanied by combustion and pyrolysis. The pyrolysis kinetics and combustion process of the energetic materials are closely related to the catalyst. They are also manifested as the combustion support or delay of the energetic materials. The ignition delay time is an important index to evaluate the combustion and pyrolysis effect of energetic materials. It is defined as the interval between the time of laser emission and the time of ignition flame signal detected by photoelectric sensor [39,40]. The ignition and combustion process of TKX-50 under the action of catalyst were studied by laser ignition and high-speed camera device (Fig. 11a ). Among them, the radiation wavelength of the laser ignition device was 10.6 μm, and the power density was 50∼350 W·cm−2 (the power density selected in this experiment was 70, 130, 190, 250, 310 W·cm−2). In order to reduce experimental errors, each group of data was tested five times in parallel and averaged.

The results showed that the delay time of all samples decreased with the increase of laser power density, and the decreasing trend slowed down with the increase of laser power. The ignition delay times of the samples tended to coincide at higher power densities (Fig. 11b). On the whole, the addition of three different catalysts all could effectively shorten the ignition delay time of TKX-50, among which CDs/Fe2O3 had the most significant reduction on the delay time, especially at low power density. This meant that the three catalysts promoted the ignition combustion of TKX-50, and the composite materials had a more prominent effect. This might be related to the drop of the initial decomposition temperature in DSC results. General studies have shown that metals and their oxide particles have different sensitivities to lasers [41,42], which can be used to explain the shortened ignition time. At low power density, when CDs and Fe2O3 acted alone, the delay time of TKX-50 was reduced to 52.8 % and 49.8 % respectively. When CDs/Fe2O3 composites were formed, the delay time was further reduced to 38.1 % of the original. This effect slowed down as the aggrandization of power density. At the higher power density (310 W·cm−2), the delay time remained basically the same. This signified that CDs/Fe2O3 effectively reduced the sensitivity of TKX-50 to laser, and greatly lessened the ignition threshold at low power density.

At a power density of 190 W·cm−2, the flame propagation images of TKX-50, TKX-50+CDs, TKX-50+Fe2O3, TKX-50+CDs/Fe2O3 were shown in Fig. 12 . The flame of pure TKX-50 was most visible and stable around 100 ms. The addition of CDs made the flame brighter and the delay time earlier. This showed that CDs was conducive to the release and transfer of energy during the combustion of TKX-50. The addition of Fe2O3 made the flame area of TKX-50 significantly increase, and the flame structure became more stable. The complete combustion time was further lower to 50∼70 ms. CDs/Fe2O3 further enhanced the flame propagation speed and flame area. It made a stable combustion at 50 ms, and the complete combustion time was reduced by half compared with TKX-50. This meant that the composite material could further improve the energy release and transfer of TKX-50 in the flame spread. The change of flame propagation velocity and flame area during ignition indicated the change of chemical combustion mechanism [36]. Therefore, CDs/Fe2O3 had a more significant improvement on the ignition performance of TKX-50.

The experimental results showed that the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 consisted of two stages, corresponding to the two exothermic peaks in DSC results. The low-temperature decomposition reaction in the first stage was the main exothermic process. The studies had shown that when the temperature reached the temperature range for low-temperature decomposition, TKX-50 first decomposed into 1,1′-ditetrazole diol (BTO) and acidic hydroxylamine (NH2OH). Then, due to the instability of NH2OH, the decomposition reaction quickly occurred and the corresponding small gas molecules (such as N2, H2O, NH3, N2O) were generated. Then the intermolecular fracture occurred in the tetrazole ring of BTO and formed the smaller unstable intermediate (1E,2E) −1,2-di(λ1-azany1) ethane-1,2‑dione dioxime (EDO) and N2. Finally, EDO was further decomposed into smaller stable polymers and some simple gas small molecules (such as CO2, CO, HCN, etc.). In the process of low-temperature decomposition, the NH3 produced by the decomposition of NH2OH would partially combine with BTO to form 5,5′-bistetrazole-1,1′-didiolate (ABTOX), and this process was reversible. The ABTOX had better thermal stability, so its formation could hinder further decomposition reactions. As the temperature increased further, ABTOX would break away from NH3 and became BTO again. Thus, the decomposition reaction continued, corresponding to the second stage of TKX-50 decomposition [11,29,35] (Fig. 13 ). In the whole process of decomposition, the generation of BTO could accelerate the decomposition of TKX-50, which played a positive feedback effect on the reaction. However, with the coming into being of BTO, the content of NH3 further rised. It aggravated the formation of by-product ABTOX, which was not conducive to the reaction [43,44].

In order to further verify the condensed phase products in the decomposition process of TKX-50, four samples before and after the addition of the catalyst were tested by in-situ solid FTIR (the test conditions were consistent with DSC). The results showed there were some differences between the decomposition temperatures measured between in-situ solid FTIR and DSC, but the overall change rules of the decomposition temperatures were consistent (Fig. 14 ). This might be due to the relatively high sensitivity of in-situ solid FTIR to changes in chemical bonds [45]. In the sample of pure TKX-50, 1574 cm−1 and 1521 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of tetrazole rings in BTO. The 2365 cm−1, 2245 cm−1 and 1690 cm−1 corresponded to the absorption peaks of NH4 + in ABTOX. In addition, 3181 cm−1 (ν OH), 1403 cm−1 (δ OH), 721 cm−1 (ν CC), 1232 cm−1 and 1162 cm−1 (ν CN) also had obvious absorption peaks. With the increase of temperature, the infrared signals corresponding to the tetrazole ring gradually weakened to disappear, corresponding to the decomposition of BTO. In addition, the absorption peaks of NH4 + appeared in the range of 240∼250℃, and the signals strength enhanced with the temperature increasing. This suggested that BTO combined with NH3 to form ABTOX.

When CDs, Fe2O3 and CDs/Fe2O3 were added, the absorption peaks of different substances did not alter significantly except the decomposition temperature was advanced. This showed that the addition of the catalyst did make the decomposition easier, and the decomposition process was without alteration. Under the action of CDs, the signal of NH4 + became less obvious, manifesting that the formation of ABTOX was inhibited during the reaction. This might be due to the fact that the surface of CDs obtained by performic acid oxidation etching. It contained a large number of carboxyl groups, which could adsorb NH3 produced after the decomposition of NH2OH, thus inhibiting the formation of ABTOX. There were more hole structures in Fe2O3, which provided numerous active centers for the decomposition of TKX-50. Therefore, the decomposition and activation energy of TKX-50 could be effectively reduced, showing good catalytic performance. This is consistent with the law of decomposition and activation energy calculated at different heating rates in DSC experiment (reducing the thermal decomposition and activation energy of TKX-50 by 24.6 kJ·mol−1). When CDs and Fe2O3 were combined to form a composite material, on the one hand, the dispersion of Fe2O3 was improved and the active site was in full contact with TKX-50, which further reduced the decomposition activation energy and promoted the decomposition reaction. On the other hand, the adsorption of CDs itself could effectively inhibit the formation of ABTOX. When the surface state functional groups of CDs were excited, there were multiple electron transitions and electron transfers between the energy gaps [21,46]. In addition, there were a large number of defects between the interface of the two components of the composite material, which could furnish the energy required for electron transition and transfer. It is favorable for the transfer of electrons [47,48]. Therefore, the composite material enables the rapid transfer of electrons to the surface and interface, facilitating the redox reaction, thus making the decomposition of TKX-50 easier.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The CDs/Fe2O3 used in solid propellants was synthesized. It was found that the CDs/Fe2O3 was irregularly spherical in size 250∼300 nm. The elements of Fe, C, O were evenly distributed.

The DSC results showed that the combination of Fe2O3 and CDs improved the dispersibility and significantly enhanced the catalytic effect on the thermal decomposition of TKX-50. Among the three catalysts studied, CDs/Fe2O3 had the best catalytic effect, which reduced the TLDP and thermal decomposition activation energy
E a
decomposition of TKX-50 by 39℃ and 40.3 kJ·mol−1. Compared with the existing catalysts, the CDs/Fe2O3 shows prominent catalytic performance of thermal decomposition.

The laser ignition experiments showed that CDs/Fe2O3 also had excellent performance in the combustion process of TKX-50. While effectively reducing the ignition delay time, it greatly increased the flame propagation speed and the stability of the flame structure, and could effectively improve the release and transmission of TKX-50 energy in the severe flame.

CDs/Fe2O3 shows good catalytic performance and is expected to be used as an efficient combustion catalyst for solid propellants containing TKX-50. On the one hand, its excellent catalytic performance came from the agglomeration of Fe2O3 and inhibiting the formation of ABTOX byproducts in the decomposition of TKX-50. On the other hand, there were more active sites in the composite, and the defects among different components were conducive to the transfer of electrons, thus promoting the redox reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Zhen Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fengqi Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Ming Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yifan Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Zhao Qin: Project administration, Formal analysis. Wengang Qu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chao Chen: Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiankan Zhang: Visualization, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
